texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stress on freshwater resources has been exacerbated in recent years, owing to factors such as population growth, urbanization, climate change and shifting lifestyles. At present, 2.1 billion people lack access to safely-managed drinking water [1]. By 2030, intense water scarcity could displace 700 million people worldwide [2]. The intricate dependence of water, food and energy security make freshwater all the more crucial for growth and prosperity. Some strategies to address water scarcity aim to mitigate demand by modifying pricing and/or implementing conservation practices. However, as Gude points out, demand mitigation approaches do little to provide solutions in areas that are severely affected [3]. In contrast, supply enhancement approaches such as desalination technologies enable us to meet growing demand by tapping into abundant water reserves in the form of seawater and/or brackish water.

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) lags behind other desalination processes such as reverse osmosis due to relatively high energy consumption. Heating in membrane distillation forms a significant component of total energy consumption. This review provides an overview of the various conventional and non-conventional heating methods used in the MD process, focusing on its application for water desalination. To make MD competitive with existing technologies, research should be directed at low-cost energy sources or alternative heating methods. Development of alternative heating methods is based on the direct heating of feed water where the driving force for distillation can be generated inside the membrane module, as is the case for photothermal heating, electrothermal heating or induction heating. Direct heating of the feed near the membrane surface can improve the thermal efficiency and economics of the MD process. Through this literature survey, we have identified some areas to guide future research in alternative heating techniques for membrane distillation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is a unique desalination process in that it is a thermally-driven membrane process. MD boasts a few advantages over conventional thermal desalination. First, MD is able to desalinate at lower temperatures, requiring less heat. Second, membrane distillation relies on a compact, modular system design as compared to conventional thermal processes. Compared to pressure-driven membrane desalination such as RO and nanofiltration (NF), MD offers the advantage of treating a range of feed types, including hypersaline feed solutions (REF). Although research in MD materials and configurations has gained momentum, there remain drawbacks that hamper wide scale commercialization of MD for water desalination.

Heating at/near the membrane surface using non-conventional methods has the potential to reduce energy costs by overcoming thermal polarization, and could be the start to developing energy-efficient MD systems. The last few years have seen a surge in the development of such alternative heating techniques.

texts.orig: reviews in recent years [6-11]. Thomas et al. provided a systematic review of the development of MD with major milestones [12]. Deshmukh et al. examined limitations to the energy efficiency, membrane design and fouling control in MD, while emphasizing opportunities that would make MD competitive with other desalination technologies [13]. Susanto reviewed the practical implementation of MD systems [14]. Several researchers have reviewed wetting, fouling and scaling phenomena [15-18], as well as modeling of the MD process [7,19-21]. Other reviews focus on membrane fabrication [22], materials [23,24], performance and cost [25-27]. However, none explore recent advances in heating techniques, especially alternative heating techniques for MD, and their implication on the energy efficiency of MD systems. Although there have been several recent reviews around MD as an attractive emerging technology for desalination, none highlight recent efforts in developing alternative techniques for heating of the membrane, membrane surface and/or spacer. This brief review assesses membrane distillation from an energy point of view, focusing on advances in alternative techniques for heating directly at the membrane and/or membrane spacer.

texts.orig: 2. Principle of membrane distillation
texts.text: 2. Principle of membrane distillation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The history of membrane distillation (MD) goes back to 1963 when it was first patented and then published in 1967 [28,29]. Nonetheless, the MD process did not attract considerable interest until the early 1980s when more efficient module membranes such as Gore-Tex became accessible [30] [3]. Membrane distillation (MD) is a thermally driven membrane separation process [6,31,32]. In a typical membrane distillation, a vapor pressure is generated across the two sides of the hydrophobic porous membrane by circulating the hot feed and cold permeate. The vapor generated on the hot feed side pass through the membrane and condenses on the cold side to produce the distillate. The vapor pressure difference increases with increase in temperature difference between the feed and the permeate. However, MD is considered a low temperature process typically operated below 80 °C. The basic schematic of the membrane distillation process is shown in 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Each configuration differs in the method by which water vapor generated on the feed side is transported and collected on the permeate side. There are four main techniques of collecting the water vapor in MD as illustrated in Fig. 2 [19,34,35].
texts.text: Each configuration differs in the method by which water vapor generated on the feed side is transported and collected on the permeate side. There are four main techniques of collecting the water vapor in MD as illustrated in Fig. 2 [19,34,35].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: I. Direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) : This is the simplest configuration in which the membrane is in direct contact with the cold water. The water vapor produced at the hot feed liquid/ membrane interface passed through the membrane pores condensed in the cold liquid/membrane interface at the permeate side. Despite the simplicity of this configuration, the conduction heat loss associated with this process is higher compared to other configurations. Due to high heat losses, DCMD has low thermal efficiency which is defined as the fraction of heat energy used only for evaporation. In DCMD, different membrane modules can be used such as hollow fiber or flat sheet under cross-flow or longitudinal flow [35-37]. It is best suited for applications such as desalination and concentration of aqueous solutions [38-40].
texts.text: Direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) : This is the simplest configuration in which the membrane is in direct contact with the cold water. The water vapor produced at the hot feed liquid/ membrane interface passed through the membrane pores condensed in the cold liquid/membrane interface at the permeate side. Despite the simplicity of this configuration, the conduction heat loss associated with this process is higher compared to other configurations. Due to high heat losses, DCMD has low thermal efficiency which is defined as the fraction of heat energy used only for evaporation. In DCMD, different membrane modules can be used such as hollow fiber or flat sheet under cross-flow or longitudinal flow [35-37]. It is best suited for applications such as desalination and concentration of aqueous solutions [38-40].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: II. Air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) : As the name suggest, an air gap is created between the membrane and a condensation surface as shown in Fig. 2 - II. Because of air gap between the membrane and the condensing surface, the heat loss is low and the configuration exhibits the highest energy efficiency, but the permeate flux obtained is generally low because of additional resistance offered to mass transfer by the air gap. By narrowing the air gap, this deficiency can be overcome to a certain extent. Since, in air gap MD, the permeate is not in direct contact with the membrane, there is no danger of membrane wetting at the permeate side. Kalla et al. [41] comprehensively reviewed the most recent developments in the AGMD process. The review covers the work done so far concerning the AGMD in order to provide a holistic view that covers the principles and applications of AGMD, effect of process parameters, membrane parameters, mathematical modeling, fouling, temperature and concentration polarization, types of membrane module, energy consumption, recent developments in AGMD process, cost estimation, and heat integration with AGMD. Warsinger and co-works study the influence of hydrophobicity of the condensing surface on the distillate production rates and thermal efficiency [42]. AGMD can be widely employed for most membrane distillation applications, particularly where energy availability is low.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: III. Sweep gas membrane distillation (SGMD) : In SGMD, an inert gas is used as a carrier for the transportation of the produced vapor to the outside condenser. The schematic of SGMD is shown in Fig. 2III. It is particularly suitable for removing volatiles from an aqueous solution [43-45]. Sweep gas in this configuration reduces the mass transfer resistance as compared to the air gap membrane distillation. However, it needs a separate condenser outside the module which add additional cost to the SGMD configuration and also a leak-proof gas system is required for this configuration.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: IV. Vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) : In this configuration, vapors on the permeate side is removed by applying the vacuum as illustrated in Fig. 2-IV and condensed outside the module. The vacuum applied on the permeate side is lower than the saturation pressure of volatile molecules to be separated from the feed solution. texts.orig: 3.1. MD configuration
texts.text: 3.1. MD configuration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from the four basic configurations of MD described above, novel configurations such as permeate gap membrane distillation (PGMD) and conductive gap membrane distillation (CGMD) have also been developed recently. PGMD is a hybrid configuration between AGMD and DCMD in which permeate fills the gap between the membrane and condensing foil. In CGMD, the gap is filled with a material with high conductivity e.g. a metal spacer. Heat loss due to conduction is considerable in configurations in which permeate is in direct contact with the membrane, as is the case for DCMD and PGMD. Comparing configurations between publications remains challenging due to nonstandardized test conditions. Studies that present direct comparison of the performance of different MD configurations are limited, and the focus of most of these studies is maximizing permeate flux, rather than minimizing energy consumption. Ding et al. [48] compared the permeate flux and selectivity for ammonia removal from water between DCMD, VMD and SGMD. In terms of mass transfer coefficient, they found that VMD > DCMD>SGMD, while in terms of selectivity, DCMD>SGMD>VMD. On the other hand, models presented by Summers et al. [47] for a simple single-stage cycle showed that high GOR is favored by DCMD and AGMD over VMD. In their design, they considered the recapture of energy to improve efficiency. The higher GOR of AGMD and DCMD resulted from local condensation of vapor, which allows heat transfer at high temperatures and improves heat recovery. They maintained that within AGMD, the spacing of the gap strongly influences energy efficiency. Cipollina et al. [50] compared AGMD, PGMD and VMD and found that the energy consumption was lowest for PGMD (Fig. 3). They attributed this to the larger vapor fluxes achieved with PGMD especially at low feed temperatures. By developing a model validated by experimental data, they designed a scaledup multi-stage unit for future testing.
texts.text: Apart from the four basic configurations of MD described above, novel configurations such as permeate gap membrane distillation (PGMD) and conductive gap membrane distillation (CGMD) have also been developed recently. PGMD is a hybrid configuration between AGMD and DCMD in which permeate fills the gap between the membrane and condensing foil. In CGMD, the gap is filled with a material with high conductivity e.g. a metal spacer. Heat loss due to conduction is considerable in configurations in which permeate is in direct contact with the membrane, as is the case for DCMD and PGMD. Comparing configurations between publications remains challenging due to nonstandardized test conditions. Studies that present direct comparison of the performance of different MD configurations are limited, and the focus of most of these studies is maximizing permeate flux, rather than minimizing energy consumption. Ding et al. [48] compared the permeate flux and selectivity for ammonia removal from water between DCMD, VMD and SGMD. In terms of mass transfer coefficient, they found that VMD > DCMD>SGMD, while in terms of selectivity, DCMD>SGMD>VMD. On the other hand, models presented by Summers et al. [47] for a simple single-stage cycle showed that high GOR is favored by DCMD and AGMD over VMD. In their design, they considered the recapture of energy to improve efficiency. The higher GOR of AGMD and DCMD resulted from local condensation of vapor, which allows heat transfer at high temperatures and improves heat recovery. They maintained that within AGMD, the spacing of the gap strongly influences energy efficiency. Cipollina et al. [50] compared AGMD, PGMD and VMD and found that the energy consumption was lowest for PGMD (Fig. 3). They attributed this to the larger vapor fluxes achieved with PGMD especially at low feed temperatures. By developing a model validated by experimental data, they designed a scaledup multi-stage unit for future testing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Studies show that a larger membrane area favors low energy consumption, however after a certain area threshold, no further increase in GOR is observed [52,53]. Criscuoli et al. evaluated the energy consumption of DCMD and VMD and found that the lowest values of energy consumption/permeate flow rate ratios were obtained for longitudinalflow DCMD and cross-flow VMD, respectively [54]. These studies highlight the need for standardized test conditions including relative flow directions and membrane area to determine the effect of configuration on GOR and energy consumption. Although membrane properties play a role, reduction in energy consumption of MD processes is likely to be driven by further development of configurations and optimization of parameters such as permeate gap and air gap. Efforts should be directed towards multi-stage MD units and energy recovery systems to lower the cost of MD. Energy recovery systems in the form of pressure retarded osmosis (PRO) and reverse electrodialysis (RED) should be employed for optimized performance with MD.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Feed temperature
texts.text: 3.2. Feed temperature

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The temperature difference across the membrane induces a vapor pressure gradient that is the driving force of any MD system. Consequently, an increase in feed temperature, or decrease in permeate temperature, increases the temperature difference across the membrane and causes an increase in vapor flux through the membrane [55]. Feed temperature is often recognized as a key parameter for controlling permeate flux through the membrane [54]. However, many studies focus on the effect of operational parameters on permeate flux alone, and the change in energy efficiency is often left out. Increasing feed temperature causes the temperature polarization coefficient to increase as the temperature difference of the bulk solutions increases. At higher bulk feed temperatures, more heat energy is used from the feed to vaporize water molecules at the membrane surface, causing a temperature drop at the surface and thus creating a difference in temperature between the solution and the membrane surface. In other words, the effect of temperature polarization becomes more pronounced at higher temperatures of the bulk feed solution [54,56]. However, the effect of increasing feed temperature on thermal efficiency remains unclear as studies have reported conflicting effects. The evaluation of a pilot-scale AGMD system showed that increasing the feed temperature from 50 to 70 °C in fact increases the thermal efficiency and also slightly decreases specific thermal energy consumption from 82 to 57 kWh/m [57]. The authors attributed the increase in permeate flux, or distillate production rate, as a factor in improved thermal efficiency [57,58]. On the other hand, Elmarghany et al. reported that increasing the feed temperature negatively affected thermal performance of DCMD due to greater heat loss from the membrane cell to the environment [59]. Thus, the effect of feed temperature on energy consumption is limited by the competing factors of increased permeate flux and increased thermal polarization. It is also possible that temperature polarization has a stronger effect in the case of DCMD where the membrane is directly in contact with the hot feed and cold permeate, and the temperature gradient at the surface differs substantially from the temperature gradient of the bulk solutions. Another limiting factors is heat loss through the membrane and from the cell, which are dependent on cell design and electrical properties of the membrane.

texts.orig: always less than the temperature gradient between the bulk liquids (Fig. 4) [23]. This phenomenon is known as temperature polarization, and is measured using the temperature polarization coefficient (TPC). Temperature polarization limits mass transport through the membrane, and the effect is enhanced in larger modules where a greater temperature drop is also observed over the membrane length [60]. One way to reduce the effect of temperature polarization and maintain the gradient across the membrane is the use of feed spacers. Net-like turbulence promoters called spacers are added near the membrane surface to improve boundary layer transfer and reduce temperature polarization [61,62]. Recently, research effort towards optimizing spacer design has boosted, with the aim of improving heat transfer and reducing the impact of temperature polarization [63,64]. However, as pointed out by Deshmukh et al. improving hydronamic mixing via the addition of spacers also has the unintended consequence of greater pressure drop in the membrane pore, which can increase the pumping energy required [13]. Another method applied to reduce the thermal boundary layer is by increasing flow rates and therefore enhancing mixing near the surface of the membrane [65].

texts.orig: However, the energy challenge remains, causing some researchers to shift their approach and focus on alternate techniques to reduce the amount of high-quality energy consumption by employing renewable energy sources and/or minimizing the effects of concentration polarization. MD research requires that energy efficiency be taken into consideration in addition to performance parameters such as permeate flux and selectivity. As heating is the single largest source of energy consumption in MD, reducing energy consumption focus on modifying existing techniques and exploring new heating techniques to control the temperature at the membrane-vapor interface.

Since then, waste heat from various sources, including exhaust gases, boiler blow down and diesel generators have been applied as the energy source for pilot and commercial MD studies [12]. Dow et al. demonstrated a DCMD pilot plant for treatment of power station wastewater over a period of three months, utilizing waste heat at 38 °C from the steam condensers of the power station. They found that the specific thermal energy consumption of running this plant is 1500 kWh/m 3 , which would make it possible for the power station to supply 8000 kL/ day. Their system produced 3 kg/m 2 h of water at a thermal efficiency of 42%. Lokare et al. integrated DCMD with waste heat from natural gas compressor stations in a simulation study using ASPEN. Their work was aimed at the treatment of produced water in Pennsylvania, USA [69]. They concluded that sufficient waste heat is available for MD treatment of all produced water in Pennsylvania. They used a minimum temperature approach to evaluate the feasibility of applying waste heat to power large-scale MD systems (see Fig. 5).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Schwantes et al. developed MD systems to treat low-salinity feeds (≤1000 ppm) driven by either solar power or waste heat. The plant powered by waste heat was designed to produce a modest capacity of 5 m 3 /day and was driven on waste heat from the cooling circuit of a diesel power station in Pantelleria, a small Mediterranean Island [70]. They found that 80% of the initial heat supply could reach the MD system, indicating high efficiency of waste heat utilization. As opposed to solar systems which require the installment of solar collector arrays, waste heat from combustion engines can appropriately supply the energy needed to operate a small-scale MD plant, provided that constant supply is available [70]. The availability of waste heat determines the simplicity and the production capacity of any given system. This means that if ample waste heat is not readily available, a system design with low specific energy consumption is necessary. On the other hand, if waste heat is cheap and readily available, low thermal efficiency is not a concern and a higher permeate flux obtained at higher specific thermal energy consumption (STEC) remains a viable option [71,72].

texts.orig: Koschikowski et al. developed solar energy driven membrane distillation (MD) system with a capacity range between 100 and 500 L/day and larger systems for the capacity range up to 10 m 3 /day [75]. The proposed systems can be operated stand-alone and almost maintenance free. Guillén-Burrieza et al. studied the solar energy driven airgap membrane distillation (AGMD) developed by the Singaporean Enterprise Keppel Seghers [76]. The AGMD module has a total surface area of 9 m 2 and tested intermittently for at least 2400 h during two years' time period. The results of this study showed they achieved best specific distillate production of as high as 5.09 L/h m 2 and heat consumption as low as 294 kWh/m 3 . Kim et al. studied solar-assisted multistage VMD (SMVMD), 24 stage, desalination system with the heat recovery and temperature modulating units [77]. The design and its performance of the system with and without a shell-and-tube heat recovery unit (HRU) was evaluated with mathematical model. In the HRU, the latent heat of condensation of the permeate vapor is recovered and supplied to the mixing junction and mixed with seawater.

texts.orig: The results indicate that integrated solar membrane distillation plant could be installed on the rooftops of residential and commercial buildings which require a continual supply of both potable water and domestic hot water. Kim et al. investigated a solar-assisted direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) system with novel energy recovery concepts for a continuous 24 h a day operation [81]. The system equipped with the heat recovery from the permeate and brine streams to the feed seawater. The solar-assisted DCMD used 50 DCMD modules having evacuated-tube collectors of 3360 m 2 . The annual solar fraction and the collector efficiency are found to be 77% and 53%, respectively, while the overall permeate production capacity is 31 m 3 /day. By using heat recovery from the brine and permeate the specific thermal energy consumption was found to be 436 kWh/m 3 which 43% lower as compared to the system without heat recovery. The specific thermal energy consumption found to be dependent on the solar collector area.
texts.text: The results indicate that integrated solar membrane distillation plant could be installed on the rooftops of residential and commercial buildings which require a continual supply of both potable water and domestic hot water. Kim et al. investigated a solar-assisted direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) system with novel energy recovery concepts for a continuous 24 h a day operation [81]. The system equipped with the heat recovery from the permeate and brine streams to the feed seawater. The solar-assisted DCMD used 50 DCMD modules having evacuated-tube collectors of 3360 m 2 . The annual solar fraction and the collector efficiency are found to be 77% and 53%, respectively, while the overall permeate production capacity is 31 m 3 /day. By using heat recovery from the brine and permeate the specific thermal energy consumption was found to be 436 kWh/m 3 which 43% lower as compared to the system without heat recovery. The specific thermal energy consumption found to be dependent on the solar collector area.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Computer simulation and modeling were also performed to estimate the performance of solar driven membrane distillation setups. Khayet et al. used Monte Carlo method to investigate the effects of operating parameters and their binary interactions on the permeate flux of sweeping gas membrane distillation in order to determine the optimum operating conditions [82]. The optimum operating conditions obtained from the model were found to be a liquid inlet temperature of 71.6 °C, a gas inlet temperature of 17.3 °C, a water circulation velocity of 0.16 m/ s (i.e. flow rate of 165 L/h) and a gas circulation velocity of 2.11 m/s (i.e. flow rate of 36 L/min). Under these optimal conditions the measured permeate flux was 2.789 × 10 -3 kg/m 2 s, while the predicted permeate flux was only 2% higher confirming the developed model. Duong et al. simulates the solar thermal driven DCMD process for smallscale seawater desalination applications [83]. The study reveals that distillate production over 140 kg of distillate each day can be achieved in a small scale DCMD module with a membrane area of 7.2 m 2 and a 22.6 m 2 flat plate solar thermal collector. This is equivalent to a daily distillate production rate of 19.7 kg/m 2 of membrane or 6.3 kg/m 2 of solar thermal collector. When they simulated the thermal efficiency along DCMD module channels, they found that thermal efficiency dropped more drastically in the case of counter-current flow from 60% to 38% between inlet and outlet feed channels, as shown in Fig. 8. They established that feed temperature had a greater effect on thermal efficiency compared to water cross flow velocity, with efficiency increasing up to 60% at a feed inlet temperature of 80 °C.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Alternative heating in membrane distillation
texts.text: 5. Alternative heating in membrane distillation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a conventional membrane distillation setup, feed is heated to a particular temperature, normally in 50-80 °C temperature, outside the membrane module by a heating coil or solar thermal heating. Due to the latent heat of vaporization, the feed temperature near the feed/membrane interface is lower than the bulk feed [6]. The phenomenon is known as temperature polarization and it negatively affects permeate flux. The limitations that arise from the inconsistent supply of waste heat, cost of renewable energy and/or possible complications in integrating waste heat or solar power systems with MD have led to the search of alternative heating techniques. We also note that although the above provide an opportunity for low-cost energy supply, they do not seek to lower the energy demand of MD systems. Although energy analysis studies have been carried out to develop MD configurations and tailor membrane materials as described in Section 3, often the role of heating the of the feed is ignored. Heating and circulating the feed still represent the largest portion of energy consumption in MD today [5]. Researchers have focused their attention to localized heating at/ near the membrane surface. Such active heating of the membrane surface or feed spacer is likely to reduce temperature polarization and enable higher recovery rates, as shown in a recent review by Deshmukh et al. [13]. Different types of heating techniques have been the focus of attention of researchers recently. These include photothermal, electrothermal, microwave and induction heating. Most publications have focused on photothermal heating for membrane distillation (Fig. 9), although the very first publications in the other heating techniques have also begun to emerge. This section reviews the innovative methods developed recently to heat membrane surface and spacers.

texts.orig: 5.1. Photothermal heating
texts.text: 5.1. Photothermal heating

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, researchers have investigated a new way heating the membrane surfaces by incorporating photothermal materials. The main photothermal materials used in MD applications are plasmonic metallic nanomaterials [86,87], inorganic semiconductor solar absorber materials [88] and carbon-based nanomaterials [89]. Many carbon-based materials exhibit photothermal behavior which allows optical absorption across a range of wavelengths and high photothermal efficiencies [90]. Metallic and semi-conductor materials that absorb light and turn it into heat efficiently do so under surface plasmon resonance (SPR), which is the wavelength at which light resonates with collective electron charge oscillations at the surface [91].
texts.text: In recent years, researchers have investigated a new way heating the membrane surfaces by incorporating photothermal materials. The main photothermal materials used in MD applications are plasmonic metallic nanomaterials [86,87], inorganic semiconductor solar absorber materials [88] and carbon-based nanomaterials [89]. Many carbon-based materials exhibit photothermal behavior which allows optical absorption across a range of wavelengths and high photothermal efficiencies [90]. Metallic and semi-conductor materials that absorb light and turn it into heat efficiently do so under surface plasmon resonance (SPR), which is the wavelength at which light resonates with collective electron charge oscillations at the surface [91].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to quantum theory, absorption of photons by bulk material leads to the excitation of electron and causes a variety of energy transformation processes [92,93] as shown in Fig. 10.
texts.text: According to quantum theory, absorption of photons by bulk material leads to the excitation of electron and causes a variety of energy transformation processes [92,93] as shown in Fig. 10.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Photothermal heating on the membrane surface can significantly increase and maintain the membrane surface temperature at the feed/ membrane interface and can drastically reduce the electricity input. Wu et al. reviewed the photothermal effect of different categories of light absorbing materials [94]. The authors also discussed the importance the photothermal materials' properties such as photothermal stability, corrosion resistance to seawater, anti-biofouling ability and environmental aspects and safety issue, material cost and long term durability. Politano and co-workers investigated the thermoplasmonic effects of gold (Ag) NPs incorporated into microporous poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) membranes to increase the feed temperature at the membrane surface by irradiating UV light [86]. The results indicate that an 11-fold increase in transmembrane permeate flux was achieved by incorporating 25% Ag NPs in PVDF membrane. The increase in permeate flux was attributed to the plasmonic effect of Ag NPs which increases the surface temperature of the membrane by > 23 K compared to the bulk temperature. In another study [95], membranes coated with carbon black nanoparticles or SiO2/Au nanoshells showed an increase of up to 33.0% in permeate flux when irradiated with simulated sunlight at 1 sun unit in a bench scale direct contact membrane distillation system.

texts.orig: Wu et al. used polydopamine (PDA) coated polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane for highly efficient solar-driven membrane distillation [96]. The results indicate that PDA coated membrane exhibited a 4.23 kg/m 2 h permeate flux under 7.0 kW/m 2 irradiation, a 19-fold enhancement over membrane without PDA coating. Tan et al. investigated the potential of MXene, a 2D material, with both photothermal and anti-fouling functionalities, as a coating to improve DCMD performance [97]. The photothermal properties of MXene help to a 12% decrease in the energy consumed by the heater per unit volume distillate. The photothermal conversion was analyzed to be 5.8 kW/m 2 , which is a few times higher than the approximately 1 kW/m 2 value provided by the sun.

texts.orig: Huang et al. used graphene graphene-based PTFE membrane for water desalination via photothermal membrane distillation (PMD) [89]. Ye et al. incorporated silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) into the hydrophobic polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibrous membranes and use ultraviolet LED to activate the plasmonic effect of nanoparticles [87]. A heating efficiency of 53 ± 7% was achieved by using this irradiation method. During the 60 h operation of MD with UV LED irradiation and 3.5 wt% NaCl feed solution, achieved a permeate flux of 2.5 kg/m 2 h and low permeate conductivity 2.65 μS/cm. Li et al. prepared photothermal membrane by decorating Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the electrospun polyvinylidenefluorideco -hexafluoropropylene (PVDFHFP) membrane using vacuum filtration [98]. The strong interfacial adhesion between Fe3O4 nanoparticles and PVDF-HFP nanofibers provide excellent performance for solar driven membrane distillation (SDMD). Solar driven MD helps to achieve a permeate flux of 0.97 kg/ m 2 h with a salt rejection rate of 99.99% under 1 kW/m 2 solar irradiation and 53% photothermal conversion efficiency was reported. The photothermal membrane was also used in a pilot-scale system and demonstrated a 21.99 kg/m 2 h which is 11% higher than when MD used without solar energy.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.2. Electrothermal heating
texts.text: 5.2. Electrothermal heating

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Joule effect is when an electric current passing through a conductive material causes power loss in the form of heat generation, thus resulting in the temperature of the material to rise [99]. Inelastic collisions between electrons in an electric field with atoms of the material cause thermal energy to be released. Heating via this mechanism is known as electrothermal heating. The terms ohmic heating and joule heating are also used. An electrothermal heater employs the Joule effect and provides a simple, controllable method of heating. Electrothermal heating has been applied to various applications including food [100], microfluidic chips [101], window defrosters [102] and deicing systems [103]. Electrothermal heating can be applied on the feed side of the membrane, by coating with an electrically conductive material, to keep the temperature at the surface high, and thus decrease temperature polarization and increase permeate flux through the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene were found to be successful candidates for the conversion of electrical energy to thermal energy. Conductive films of carbon-based nanostructured materials have the advantage of being light-weight and flexible [104], and can be used for efficient and uniform heating [105]. Thus these materials have been used as electrothermal heaters in a several recent studies [106-113]. Very few researchers have assessed the use of CNT membranes for electrothermal heating in MD, owing to the instability of the film in ionizable media such as water, under the effect of an electric field. The use of electrothermal films for desalination and water treatment is limited by the difficulty and instability of novel electrothermal heaters in ionizable media such as water. The challenge consists of two parts: developing an appropriate membrane, and optimizing operation parameters particularly the voltage and frequency. To achieve rapid electrothermal heating in water, a robust conductive film heater that does not hinder membrane performance is a key requirement.

texts.orig: Dudchenko et al. [114] indicated that a DC voltage is not adequate to heat CNT films in ionizable media as very large electric potentials are needed for heating due to losses to the water, and such high potentials could lead to degradation of the CNT membrane. They were first group to apply electrothermal heating to MD. In their work, they found that CNT membranes can be heated in highly saline aqueous environments [114]. Subsequently, Li et al. [115] coated graphene oxide on a commercial polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane in the air gap of AGMD. They referred to this configuration as reverse Joule-heating AGMD. Interestingly, the conductive layer, and thus the heating, was not on the feed side, but on the cooler side, thus creating an opposite temperature gradient (Fig. 11b). As compared to conventional AGMD in which permeate flux declined with time (negative slope of -0.0035), permeate flux for reverse Joule-heating AGMD remained stable (negligibly positive slope of 0.0009), due to minimization of capillary condensation or wetting (Fig. 11a).

texts.orig: Although both studies demonstrate electrothermal heating at membrane surface to eliminate bulk feed heating in MD, they both applied small cells, in the millimeter range. Such a study needs to be employed to a larger membrane area to assess its feasibility and energy efficiency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Alsaati and Marconnet also applied a locally heated MD approach in which an electrically conductive silver membrane is heated and mass transfer takes place across the heated porous membrane into stagnant air [116]. They heated a silver membrane using an external heater. Compared to DCMD and AGMD, their approach demonstrated a 75% reduction of energy. In a recent study [117], we proposed a hybrid heating where an electrically conductive carbon nanostructures (CNS) layer is coated on commercial polypropylene (PP) membranes. CNS are essentially multi-walled CNTs with a high aspect ratio, and a large number of defects that enable rapid low-cost production [118]. By applying AC bias across the CNS layer, localized heating of the membrane surface through the electrothermal heating effect was achieved, which in turn improved transmembrane permeate flux. The schematic of the electrothermal heating method is shown in Fig. 12.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feed was circulated at three different temperatures 40, 50 and 60 °C with and without 24 V AC potential to evaluate the effect of applied potential on MD performance. In all cases, permeate flux through the membrane increased significantly when a potential was applied. For feed temperatures of 40 and 50 °C, the applied potential enhanced the average permeate flux by > 75%, whereas at a feed temperature of 60 °C, a 61% improvement in permeate flux was observed between no potential and 24 V. At 40C, electrothermal heating reduced SEC to 1 kWh/kg. Electrothermal heating of the membrane surface with applied potential reduces the effects of temperature polarization on the feed side. This study opens up new ways to improve the energy efficiency of the MD process. Electrothermal heating in MD is still in its infancy with much room for improvement in system design and energy efficiency.

texts.orig: 5.4. Induction heating
texts.text: 5.4. Induction heating

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In electromagnetic induction, an alternating magnetic field is generated by means of flowing an ac current through a coil. This alternating magnetic field cuts through an electrically conductive material that is to be heated and induces eddy currents, which then dissipate energy and cause the temperature of the material to rise [127]. The heating rate and uniformity depend on factors such as coil design and selection of power supply rating and frequency. Induction heating is widely used in industrial, domestic and medical applications [128].
texts.text: In electromagnetic induction, an alternating magnetic field is generated by means of flowing an ac current through a coil. This alternating magnetic field cuts through an electrically conductive material that is to be heated and induces eddy currents, which then dissipate energy and cause the temperature of the material to rise [127]. The heating rate and uniformity depend on factors such as coil design and selection of power supply rating and frequency. Induction heating is widely used in industrial, domestic and medical applications [128].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anvari et al. applied induction heating in VMD for treating highsalinity feeds (between 35 and 100 g/L NaCl), by generating a highfrequency magnetic field. They developed a membrane suitable for induction heating by spray coating iron oxide-CNT on a hydrophobic PTFE commercial membrane [129]. A heating coil of frequency 283 kHz was placed under the membrane module for heating the membrane on the feed side. They found that increasing the thickness of the coated layer increased the rate of membrane heating. They used an optimum thickness of 30 μm to increase the driving force without adding significantly to mass transfer resistance. Although induction heating increased the permeate flux in all cases, a higher flux of 4 kg/ m 2 was achieved at lower feed flow velocity (2.33 cm/min) as compared to 2.8 kg/m 2 at a feed velocity of 11.25 cm/min. At higher feed velocity, there is less time for the feed to be in contact with the heated membrane, leading to less heating of the feed at the membrane surface, and thus a lower driving force for MD. Fig. 14 compares the permeate flux and specific thermal energy consumption for conventional MD and for induction heated VMD (IH-VMD) [129].

texts.orig: Zen Tan et al. [97] investigated the feasibility of localized induction heating of metallic Ni for improving the energy efficiency of DCMD, and compared their system with conventional DCMD using simulations and experiments. They found that induction heating resulted in increased permeate flux and energy efficiency than conventional external heating and insulating mesh. The effect on permeate flux was pronounced at lower feed flow rates. When compared with polypropylene (PP) mesh, the Ni foam performed worse at lower feed temperature due to less heat conduction through the spacer and poorer contact between spacer and membrane. They found that induction heating could also be used to supplement external heating without a decrease in energy efficiency, which would allow to compensate for temporal fluctuations in sustainable energy sources such as waste heat and solar.

texts.orig: 6. Economical considerations and upscaling
texts.text: 6. Economical considerations and upscaling

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are many aspects to consider in the upscaling of direct membrane heating via the techniques described in Section 5. First, we should consider the barriers to commercial breakthrough of membrane distillation in general: energy consumption and cost [134]. The thermal energy efficiency of an MD system is recognized as a key factor in its energy consumption, and, indirectly, in the commercialization of MD. A multistage system design is common to other desalination technologies including multi-stage flash (MSF) and RO and has played a role in the maturity of these technologies by increasing efficiency. In MD, such multi-stage designs are only recently being investigated [135-139]. Patel et al. argue that the energy efficiency of desalination technologies is not likely to improve via advanced material design, and that researchers need to take on an approach that focuses on system design

The next aspect to consider for upscaling is membrane fabrication. CNTs and graphene-based materials are at the forefront of most direct heating studies carried out for MD, including photothermal, electrothermal and induction heating. Once considered expensive, these materials can now be fabricated in large quantities at relatively low costs, from a wide range of carbon precursors [141], making it feasible to prepare commercial-scale membranes for direct heating.

texts.orig: Anvari et al. performed a simulation to study the scaling up of their induction heated IH-VMD system using a finite element method (FEM) approach. They found that increasing the module size for the IH-VMD system led to a higher flux as the feed was always in contact with a hot membrane surface. This is in contrast to conventional VMD, in which the temperature of the feed decreases along the module length as a result of heat loss due to water evaporation and contact with the cooler membrane surface [129], and the specific thermal energy to distill 1 kg of water is ~0.5 kWh at a heat flux of 4000 W/m 2 . This behavior was observed even at high feed flow velocities of up to 1532.5 cm/min (Fig. 15).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Direct heating techniques provide greater control over the temperature at the membrane surface, by means of controlling the driving force for heating. For example, the voltage applied in the case of electrothermal heating is a key parameter in determining system performance at a larger scale. For electrothermal heating, no modification apart from external connections is needed, making direct heating a simple technique to implement and operate for enhanced MD. Scaling up of direct heating depends on the specific heating technique used. For example, closely packed spiral wound or hollow fiber membrane modules cannot be easily irradiated with UV or solar energy in large areas [13]. This will hamper their use for large-scale systems. On the other hand, electrothermal heating may be applied with ease to membranes in dense configurations, making the prospect of electrothermal heating more scalable in the near future. However, optimization of the voltage and the properties of the electrothermal membrane are needed before considering commercialization.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Conclusion
texts.text: 7. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The advent of direct heating for membrane distillation through selfheating membranes and spacers could revolutionize the future of the technology. Although membrane distillation has several applications, the most prominent is desalination. Membrane distillation shows strong potential in areas where other desalination technologies lack, such as treatment of hypersaline brine. One of the major impediments for membrane distillation has been energy consumption. Although MD operates at much lower temperatures than conventional thermal desalination, the cost of thermal energy still makes up a large portion of the overall energy consumption. The two solutions to this are (1) using lowcost energy sources, and (2) reducing the thermal energy required to run an MD system. Traditionally, waste heat and solar power have been considered as low-cost energy sources for MD. However, the temporal fluctuations and lack of abundant supply in many areas requires alternative sources to be considered. Additionally, the energy consumption is closely tied to the thermal efficiency, which is often lowered due to the phenomenon of temperature polarization. Direct heating of the membrane or spacer work by keeping the surface temperature hot and maintaining the driving force for distillation. This favorable decrease in temperature polarization is brought about by developing smart membranes or spacers with self-heating, and applying these to various MD configurations and evaluating the effect on membrane performance and energy efficiency. To date, most studies surrounding direct heating have focused on photothermal materials. However, due to the large areas required for irradiation, photothermal heating may be limited to small-scale standalone MD desalination systems. Researchers have also demonstrated other direct heating techniques such as electrothermal heating and induction heating by coating a layer of conductive material on the membrane. It is a challenge to coat the membrane without affecting its desirable properties such as hydrophobicity and appropriate pore size. Thus, newer research is targeted towards self-heating spacers, that do not interfere with membrane properties, yet provide heating close to the membrane surface.

table_cells.text: The simplest configuration to realize practically; flux is more stable than VMD for the feeds with fouling tendency, high gained output ratio, it might be the most appropriate configuration for removal of volatile components.

Flux obtained is relatively lower than vacuum configurations under the identical operating conditions, thermal polarization is highest among all the configurations, flux is relatively more sensitive to feed concentration, the permeate quality is sensitive to membrane wetting, suitable mainly for aqueous solutions.

High flux, can be used for recovery of aroma compounds and related substances, the permeate quality is stable despite of some wetting, no possibility of wetting from distillate side, thermal polarization is very low.
Higher probability of pore wetting, higher fouling, minimum selectivity of volatile components, require vacuum pump and external condenser.
Relatively high flux, low thermal losses, no wetting on permeate side, less fouling tendency.
Air gap provides an additional resistance to vapors, difficult module designing, difficult to model due to the involvement of too many variables, lowest gained output ratio.
Thermal polarization is lower, no wetting from permeate side, permeate quality independent of membrane wetting
Additional complexity due to the extra equipment involved, heat recovery is difficult, low flux, pretreatment of sweep gas might be needed
